paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: Most of us spend a lot of our time feeling a subtle pressure to know things, to understand our world so that we can make predictions, feel safe, and be seen as knowledgeable. But the moment we think we know everything is also the moment we stop learning. What if there's always more to the story than we can ever know? This is the practice of wonder, the W in view.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Wonder is curiosity without looking for an answer. When you're looking for an answer, your system constricts a little bit. But if you're just like, oh my gosh, what is happening here? And there's no pressure to find an answer, then the physical state remains expansive. It's like curiosity and awe put together. If you're awestruck by something, you have a recognition that it's out of your control, something beyond your ability to maybe even recognize in that moment.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: The other thing that's particularly important is that you're in the question. There was a time in my life when this question arose: what am I? I was in that question for 10 years. It wasn't about trying to answer the question, it was about being in the question. I could come up with answers, but every answer was based on some context. The power of that was being in the question, it wasn't ever finding the answer to the question.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: If you just think about those times that you're awestruck, imagine a life when you're awestruck all the time. We're experiencing miracles all the time, but we're so used to them. If you really just contemplate why sun, why universe, why cosmos, it's awe-inspiring. I was listening to the head of Amazon recently who talked about the beginning of his day, he would just wander. There's a harvest that comes from wandering, or from being in wonder, because you discover all sorts of cool stuff.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: In today's world, it's really not the answer. In the age of Google, anybody can find an answer. It's really the right question. Being in wonder helps you ask the right questions. One person can spend their life building Google, and another person can spend their life building the local tire shop. It's just a different question that they're living in.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: The opposite of wonder is knowing or wanting to be seen as knowing. I remember as a venture capitalist, people would come to me and try to impress me with their knowledge. But if they come in with wonder, like one CEO who asked, what do you need to see to fund? What are the boxes that you need to check off? He started in a place of wonder instead of a place of knowing. We like to be in a learning journey with each other.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Questioning the assumption that's in your mind is one of the quickest ways to get to wonder. If I say to you, life is challenging because I don't have enough work, there's so many assumptions in that. There's an assumption that it's challenging not to have enough work. There could be other assumptions like, oh, wow, I have free time to start my own business. What makes you think your truth is truth everywhere or even true for you right now?

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Last night, we took our girls out to a stunning lake in Arizona. When we were driving back, there was a steakhouse with political signs that were in contrast with my daughter's political beliefs. I said, okay, we're going to go eat there. One was scared and one was like, no, I don't want to deal with these people. I was saying, well, how is that any different than racism? As we were driving home, I heard both of my daughters see some of the wisdom on the other side of the political argument. Their heart opened up because they were in wonder about this whole situation.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Being in wonder doesn't mean that you're not clear on the actions that you're taking. I know in my business what the next step is. I don't know that it's going to be the right step. I don't know that a better next step isn't going to show up a second from now. There's a knowing of my truth, there's a knowing of a process that works. But it stops me from thinking that I know.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: In college, I had the honor of being kicked out of my first college. I had a 3.95 grade point average and getting kicked out at the same time. That could only be done if I was in knowing instead of wonder.
